claim_chain: IOH prediction models often rely on high-resolution waveforms not routinely available
We developed a Transformer-based model using only vital sign time-series data
The model demonstrated strong predictive performance across multiple time horizons
IOH burden was significantly associated with postoperative AKI and AKD
The model provides an accurate, generalizable, and actionable solution for perioperative hemodynamic management
Prospective validation is needed before clinical implementation

typical_pattern: Provide supporting evidence with specific metrics
Compare with existing approaches or literature
Conclude with implications or future directions

likely_priorities: Prospective validation and real-time clinical applicability
Comparison with models incorporating richer clinical inputs
Clinical utility and actionability of short-term predictions
Causal relationship between IOH and postoperative outcomes
likely_attacks: Vital-signs-only approach excludes important clinical factors like drug dosing and fluid administration
Short-term prediction may not translate to improved clinical outcomes
Limited external validation from only one additional site
Computational requirements may limit real-world deployment
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly acknowledges its retrospective design as a limitation and states that prospective, multicenter validation is needed. It extensively defends the vital-signs-only approach against criticisms that it excludes anesthetic dosing, fluid administration, and surgical complexity, citing scalability and real-world feasibility. The authors address debates about the clinical value of short-term IOH prediction by incorporating a nested cohort analysis and discussing prior RCTs that showed limited AKI benefit despite reducing hypotension. They also discuss computational efficiency concerns and compare inference times between models.

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, author names, or invented numbers were included. The profile is based solely on the provided abstract, discussion, and figure legends.
